scene_title: A chamber in a farmhouse adjoining the castle

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Here is better than the open air; take it thankfully. I will

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: piece out the comfort with what addition I can: I will not

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: be long from you.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: All the power of his wits have given way to his impatience:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: the gods reward your kindness!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Frateretto calls me; and tells me

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Nero is an angler in the lake of darkness.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Pray, innocent, and beware the foul fiend.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Prithee, nuncle, tell me whether a madman be a gentleman or a yeoman?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: A king, a king!

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: No, he’s a yeoman that has a gentleman to his son; for

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: he’s a mad yeoman that sees his son a gentleman before him.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To have a thousand with red burning spits

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Come hissing in upon ’em—

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: The foul fiend bites my back.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: He’s mad that trusts in the tameness of a wolf, a horse’s

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: health, a boy’s love, or a whore’s oath.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: It shall be done; I will arraign them straight.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To Edgar. Come, sit thou here, most learned justicer;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To the Fool. Thou, sapient sir, sit here. Now, you she foxes!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Look, where he stands and glares!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Wantest thou eyes at trial, madam?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Come o’er the bourn, Bessy, to me—

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Her boat hath a leak,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: And she must not speak

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Why she dares not come over to thee.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: The foul fiend haunts poor Tom in the voice of a nightingale.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Hopdance cries in Tom’s belly for two white herring. Croak not, black

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: angel; I have no food for thee.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: How do you, sir? Stand you not so amazed:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Will you lie down and rest upon the cushions?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I’ll see their trial first. Bring in the evidence.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To Edgar. Thou robed man of justice, take thy place;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To the Fool. And thou, his yoke-fellow of equity,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Bench by his side: To Kent. you are o’ the commission,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Sit you too.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Let us deal justly.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Sleepest or wakest thou, jolly shepherd?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Thy sheep be in the corn;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: And for one blast of thy minikin mouth,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Thy sheep shall take no harm.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Pur! the cat is gray.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Arraign her first; ’tis Goneril. I here take my

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: oath before this honourable assembly, she kicked the

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: poor king her father.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Come hither, mistress. Is your name Goneril?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: She cannot deny it.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Cry you mercy, I took you for a joint-stool.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And here’s another, whose warp’d looks proclaim

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What store her heart is made on. Stop her there!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Arms, arms, sword, fire! Corruption in the place!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: False justicer, why hast thou let her ’scape?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Bless thy five wits!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: O pity! Sir, where is the patience now,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That thou so oft have boasted to retain?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: My tears begin to take his part so much,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: They’ll mar my counterfeiting.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The little dogs and all, Tray, Blanch, and

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Sweet-heart, see, they bark at me.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Tom will throw his head at them. Avaunt, you curs!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Be thy mouth or black or white,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Tooth that poisons if it bite;

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Mastiff, grey-hound, mongrel grim,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Hound or spaniel, brach or lym,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Or bobtail tike or trundle-tail,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Tom will make them weep and wail:

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: For, with throwing thus my head,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Dogs leap the hatch, and all are fled.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Then let them anatomize Regan; see what breeds about her heart. Is

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: there any cause in nature that makes these hard hearts? To Edgar.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You, sir, I entertain for one of my hundred; only I do

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: not like the fashion of your garments: you will say they are

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Persian attire: but let them be changed.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Now, good my lord, lie here and rest awhile.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Make no noise, make no noise; draw the curtains: so, so, so.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: We’ll go to supper i’ he morning. So, so, so.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: And I’ll go to bed at noon.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Come hither, friend: where is the king my master?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Here, sir; but trouble him not, his wits are gone.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Good friend, I prithee, take him in thy arms;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I have o’erheard a plot of death upon him:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: There is a litter ready; lay him in ’t,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And drive towards Dover, friend, where thou shalt meet

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Both welcome and protection. Take up thy master:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If thou shouldst dally half an hour, his life,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: With thine, and all that offer to defend him,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Stand in assured loss: take up, take up;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And follow me, that will to some provision

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Give thee quick conduct.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Oppressed nature sleeps:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: This rest might yet have balm’d thy broken senses,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Which, if convenience will not allow,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Stand in hard cure.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To the Fool. Come, help to bear thy master;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Thou must not stay behind.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Come, come, away.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: When we our betters see bearing our woes,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: We scarcely think our miseries our foes.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Who alone suffers suffers most i’ the mind,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Leaving free things and happy shows behind:

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: But then the mind much sufferance doth o’er skip,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: When grief hath mates, and bearing fellowship.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: How light and portable my pain seems now,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: When that which makes me bend makes the king bow,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: He childed as I father’d! Tom, away!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Mark the high noises; and thyself bewray,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: When false opinion, whose wrong thought defiles thee,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: In thy just proof, repeals and reconciles thee.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: What will hap more to-night, safe ’scape the king!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Lurk, lurk.

stage_directions.text: Enter Gloucester, King Lear, Kent, Fool, and Edgar.
Do de, de, de. Sessa! Come, march to wakes and fairs and market-towns. Poor Tom, thy horn is dry.

glosses.definition: supplement; fill in
small landholder — Lear on class and nightcaps
put them on trial immediately — Lear's command begins the mock trial (Q1-only)
Lear's mad-play court: Poor Tom as judge
partner in justice — the Fool
Lear addresses a stool as Goneril in the mad trial
Regan as the second stool-defendant
joint-stool (a plain bench) — the Fool jests that Lear has 'taken her for a join-stool'
corrupt judge — Lear's cry when Regan 'escapes'
mongrel dog
dissect her — Lear's ferocious curiosity
bed-hangings — Lear imagines himself in a royal bed
exotic clothing — Lear's playful reassignment of Tom's rags
demon from Harsnett's 1603 Declaration — another named devil
Poor Tom's demon
judge — formal trial vocabulary applied to a farmhouse bench
court deputation
bribery — Lear's accusation against his own hallucinated court
bad judge — Lear's rage when Regan 'escapes'
mongrel with a docked tail — insults of Tom's demon-repertoire
another mongrel variety
stretcher
Kent addressing the Fool directly

annotations.title: The mock trial — Q1-only material absent from the Folio
annotations.body: The mock trial of Goneril and Regan — in which Lear, Kent, the Fool, and Edgar (as Poor Tom) hold court over two joint-stools standing in for the absent sisters — exists ONLY in the 1608 Quarto of King Lear (Q1). The 1623 Folio (F1) omits it entirely. This is one of the most significant Q1/F1 divergences in Shakespeare, and a test-case for editorial philosophy. Conflated editions (including Standard Ebooks' text) keep the trial. Some modern scholars (Gary Taylor, Michael Warren, Stanley Wells) argue that Q1 and F1 are different plays that Shakespeare revised, and that the Folio's excision of the mock trial was a deliberate late choice — tightening the scene, cutting a passage whose absurdity may have played better as read than staged. Whatever the textual politics, a reader encountering the conflated text encounters a scene Shakespeare may have later wanted removed. That layered presence matters: the trial is both definitively Shakespearean and potentially something he reconsidered.
sources: Gary Taylor and Michael Warren (eds.), The Division of the Kingdoms (1983)
Stanley Wells and Gary Taylor, William Shakespeare: A Textual Companion (1987)

annotations.title: Putting the daughters on trial — Lear's attempt to legislate grief
annotations.body: Lear's mock court is a desperate attempt to do formally what the real justice system refuses: try Goneril and Regan for ingratitude. The scene has the structure of a legal proceeding (judges, prosecution, evidence) performed over absent defendants represented by furniture. It is bleakly funny and unbearably sad at once. The Fool's 'Come hither, mistress. Is your name Goneril?' addressing a joint-stool ratifies the theatrical convention. That Lear demands he 'arraign them straight' reveals the category-collapse: he believes the theater of the trial is itself justice. When Regan 'escapes' ('Stop her there!'), Lear screams of 'false justicers' — and is right in a way he does not mean. The play has taught by 3.6 that the court system cannot touch Goneril and Regan, so Lear improvises a parallel theater in the farmhouse. That this scene was later cut from the Folio may be because its bleakness pushed past what even Jacobean tragic convention could bear.

annotations.title: The Fool's disappearance — an unresolved crux
annotations.body: After 3.6 the Fool vanishes from the play. He has no death scene, no exit announcement, no reference by other characters. Scholars have offered several explanations: (1) the doubling theory — the same actor played Cordelia and the Fool in the original production, making the Fool's absence after 3.6 a theatrical necessity (Cordelia returns in 4.4); (2) the dying-offstage theory — Lear's later 'my poor fool is hanged' in 5.3 may refer equivocally to both Cordelia and the Fool; (3) the deliberate-silence theory — Shakespeare leaves the Fool's fate unexplained because the play is testing how much absence an audience can bear. The first theory is probably correct practically; the third is dramaturgically interesting. Either way, the Fool's disappearance is a reminder that the play's theater has working constraints (actor-count) that shaped its text.
sources: Richard Abrams, 'The Double Casting of Cordelia and the Fool,' Text and Performance Quarterly (1985)
Stephen Booth, King Lear, Macbeth, Indefinition, and Tragedy (1983)

trials.prompt: The mock trial of Goneril and Regan in 3.6 is textually significant because —
options: It is the longest scene in the play
It exists ONLY in the 1608 Quarto (Q1); the 1623 Folio (F1) omits it entirely — one of the most important Q1/F1 divergences in the Shakespeare canon
It was added by an editor

trials.prompt: Why does Lear stage a mock trial on the heath?
options: To improvise a parallel justice system because the real one cannot or will not try Goneril and Regan — Lear legislating grief when law has failed him

trials.prompt: What happens to the Fool after 3.6?
options: He vanishes without explanation — theories include actor-doubling with Cordelia, offstage death implied by Lear's 'my poor fool is hanged,' or deliberate authorial silence; the crux is unresolved
